questiontext.text: A bounded function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is Riemann integrable if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The set of discontinuities of \(f\) has Lebesgue measure zero

Lebesgue's criterion: \(f\) is Riemann integrable on \([a,b]\) iff it is bounded and its set of discontinuities has measure zero (i.e., \(f\) is continuous almost everywhere). Continuous functions, monotone functions (at most countably many discontinuities), and bounded functions with finitely many discontinuities are all Riemann integrable. The Dirichlet function (discontinuous everywhere) is not Riemann integrable.

answer.text: The set of discontinuities of \(f\) has Lebesgue measure zero

name.text: Riemann Integration - Upper and Lower Darboux Sums

questiontext.text: For a bounded function \(f\) on \([a,b]\) and any partition \(P\), it is always true that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(L(f,P) \leq L(f,Q)\) for any refinement \(Q \supseteq P\)

Key property of Darboux sums: refining a partition increases lower sums and decreases upper sums. Formally: if \(Q\) is a refinement of \(P\), then \(L(f,P) \leq L(f,Q) \leq U(f,Q) \leq U(f,P)\). Consequently, for any two partitions \(P_1, P_2\): \(L(f,P_1) \leq U(f,P_2)\) (lower sums always \(\leq\) upper sums, regardless of partition). The function is Riemann integrable iff \(\inf_P U(f,P) = \sup_P L(f,P)\).

name.text: Riemann Integration - Fundamental Theorem Part I

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(F\) is Lipschitz (hence uniformly continuous) on \([a,b]\), and \(F'(x)=f(x)\) at every point of continuity of \(f\)

FTC Part 1: \(F\) is always Lipschitz continuous (since \(|F(x)-F(y)|\leq M|x-y|\) where \(|f|\leq M\)). Moreover, \(F'(x)=f(x)\) at every point where \(f\) is continuous. \(F\) may not be differentiable at discontinuities of \(f\) (though Darboux's theorem says \(F'\) has IVP). If \(f\) is merely integrable (not necessarily continuous), \(F\) need not be differentiable everywhere.

answer.text: \(F\) is uniformly continuous on \([a,b]\), and \(F'(x)=f(x)\) at every continuity point of \(f\)
\(F\) is differentiable except on the set where \(f\) is discontinuous, but \(F'\) could be 0 there
\(F\) is constant if \(f\) has a discontinuity

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\pi/4\)

Using the identity \(\sin^2(x) = (1-\cos(2x))/2\): \(\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^2 x\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{1-\cos(2x)}{2}dx = \frac{1}{2}\left[x - \frac{\sin(2x)}{2}\right]_0^{\pi/2} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\sin\pi}{2}\right) - 0 = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\). Alternatively, use the reduction formula or Wallis's formula.

Answer: (C) \(p > 1\)

\(\int_1^{\infty} x^{-p}\,dx = \left[\frac{x^{1-p}}{1-p}\right]_1^{\infty}\) for \(p\neq1\). For \(p>1\): \(x^{1-p}\to0\) as \(x\to\infty\), so integral \(= \frac{0-(1)}{1-p} = \frac{1}{p-1}\). For \(p<1\): integral diverges (goes to \(\infty\)). For \(p=1\): \(\int_1^{\infty}dx/x = \ln x\big|_1^{\infty} = \infty\). Convergence iff \(p>1\), mirroring the \(p\)-series test.

questiontext.text: The Gamma function \(\Gamma(n) = \displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} t^{n-1}e^{-t}\,dt\) satisfies \(\Gamma(n) =\) for positive integers \(n\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \((n-1)!\)

By integration by parts: \(\Gamma(n) = \int_0^\infty t^{n-1}e^{-t}dt\). Setting \(u=t^{n-1}, dv=e^{-t}dt\): \(\Gamma(n) = (n-1)\int_0^\infty t^{n-2}e^{-t}dt = (n-1)\Gamma(n-1)\). Since \(\Gamma(1)=\int_0^\infty e^{-t}dt=1=0!\), induction gives \(\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!\). Also: \(\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt{\pi}\), \(\Gamma(3/2)=\sqrt{\pi}/2\). The functional equation \(\Gamma(z+1)=z\Gamma(z)\) extends to all complex \(z\) except non-positive integers.

Answer: (A) \(\dfrac{\Gamma(m)\Gamma(n)}{\Gamma(m+n)}\)

The fundamental relation \(B(m,n) = \Gamma(m)\Gamma(n)/\Gamma(m+n)\) connects the Beta and Gamma functions. For positive integers: \(B(m,n) = (m-1)!(n-1)!/(m+n-1)!\). Special values: \(B(1/2,1/2) = \Gamma(1/2)^2/\Gamma(1) = \pi\). The Beta function also appears as \(\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2m-1}(\theta)\cos^{2n-1}(\theta)\,d\theta = B(m,n)/2\).

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet function \(D(x) = \begin{cases}1 & x \in \mathbb{Q} \\ 0 & x \notin \mathbb{Q}\end{cases}\) on \([0,1]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Not Riemann integrable, but Lebesgue integrable with \(\int D\,dx = 0\)

For any partition \(P\): each subinterval contains both rationals (where \(D=1\)) and irrationals (where \(D=0\)). So \(U(D,P) = 1\) and \(L(D,P) = 0\) for every partition. Thus \(\overline{\int}D = 1 \neq 0 = \underline{\int}D\), so \(D\) is NOT Riemann integrable. However, \(\mathbb{Q}\) has Lebesgue measure zero, so the Lebesgue integral \(\int_{[0,1]} D\,d\mu = 0\).

answer.text: Not Riemann integrable, but Lebesgue integrable with integral 0

questiontext.text: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x e^x\,dx\) using integration by parts:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(1\)

Let \(u=x, dv=e^x dx\), so \(du=dx, v=e^x\). Then \(\int xe^x dx = xe^x - \int e^x dx = xe^x - e^x + C = (x-1)e^x + C\). Evaluating: \([(x-1)e^x]_0^1 = (1-1)e^1 - (0-1)e^0 = 0 - (-1) = 1\).

name.text: Riemann Integration - Comparison for Improper Integrals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Converges

Near \(x=0\): \(\sin x \approx x\), so \(\frac{\sin x}{x^{3/2}} \approx \frac{x}{x^{3/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} = x^{-1/2}\). Since \(\int_0^1 x^{-1/2}dx = 2\) converges, and \(0 \leq \frac{\sin x}{x^{3/2}} \leq \frac{x}{x^{3/2}} = x^{-1/2}\) for \(x\in(0,1]\), by comparison test the integral converges. The singularity at 0 is integrable since the order is \(-1/2 > -1\).

name.text: Riemann Integration - Integrability of Monotone Functions

questiontext.text: A monotone bounded function \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) is Riemann integrable because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Its set of discontinuities is countable (hence has measure zero)

A monotone function on \([a,b]\) has at most countably many discontinuities (all jump type). A countable set has Lebesgue measure zero. By Lebesgue's criterion, the function is Riemann integrable. One can also prove this directly: for any \(\epsilon>0\), partition \([a,b]\) into \(n\) equal parts with width \(\delta=(b-a)/n\); then \(U-L \leq (f(b)-f(a))\delta = (f(b)-f(a))(b-a)/n < \epsilon\) for large \(n\).

answer.text: Its set of discontinuities is at most countable, hence has Lebesgue measure zero
Monotone functions are always differentiable almost everywhere

questiontext.text: If \(f\) and \(g\) are Riemann integrable on \([a,b]\) with \(f(x) \leq g(x)\) for all \(x\in[a,b]\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\displaystyle\int_a^b f \leq \int_a^b g\)

Monotonicity of the Riemann integral: if \(f\leq g\), then \(\int_a^b f\,dx \leq \int_a^b g\,dx\). This follows because \(g-f\geq0\), and for non-negative integrable functions, the integral is non-negative (since lower Darboux sums are non-negative for non-negative functions). This is fundamental for integral inequalities and comparison arguments.

answer.text: No comparison is possible without continuity

name.text: Riemann Integration - Mean Value Theorem for Integrals

questiontext.text: If \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) is continuous, the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees a point \(c \in (a,b)\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(f(c) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\)

MVT for integrals: if \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(\exists c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)(b-a) = \int_a^b f\,dx\), i.e., \(f(c)\) equals the average value \(\frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f\). Proof: let \(F(x)=\int_a^x f\), which is differentiable (FTC), and apply MVT to \(F\): \(F(b)-F(a) = F'(c)(b-a) = f(c)(b-a)\).

questiontext.text: Thomae's function (defined as \(1/q\) for \(x=p/q\) in lowest terms, 0 for irrationals) on \([0,1]\) is Riemann integrable because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) It is continuous at every irrational and its set of discontinuities (the rationals) has measure zero

Thomae's function is discontinuous precisely at every rational in \([0,1]\) (countably many), hence a set of Lebesgue measure zero. By Lebesgue's criterion, it is Riemann integrable. Its Riemann integral equals 0 (matching its Lebesgue integral, since the function equals 0 a.e.). This shows bounded functions with countably many discontinuities are Riemann integrable.

answer.text: Its discontinuities form a countable (measure-zero) set

name.text: Riemann Integration - Differentiation Under the Integral Sign

questiontext.text: If \(F(t) = \displaystyle\int_a^b f(x,t)\,dx\) and \(f, \partial f/\partial t\) are continuous, then \(F'(t) =\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\displaystyle\int_a^b \frac{\partial f}{\partial t}(x,t)\,dx\)

Leibniz integral rule (differentiation under the integral sign): under the stated conditions (continuity of \(f\) and \(\partial f/\partial t\) on \([a,b]\times[c,d]\)), differentiation and integration can be interchanged: \(F'(t) = \int_a^b \partial f/\partial t\,dx\). More generally, if limits depend on \(t\): \(\frac{d}{dt}\int_{g(t)}^{h(t)} f(x,t)\,dx = f(h(t),t)h'(t) - f(g(t),t)g'(t) + \int_{g(t)}^{h(t)}\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\,dx\).

answer.text: Cannot be differentiated without more conditions

questiontext.text: Using the reduction formula \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^n x\,dx\), Wallis's product gives:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\dfrac{\pi}{2} = \displaystyle\prod_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(2n)(2n)}{(2n-1)(2n+1)}\)

Wallis's product: \(\frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{2}{1}\cdot\frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{4}{3}\cdot\frac{4}{5}\cdot\frac{6}{5}\cdot\frac{6}{7}\cdots = \prod_{n=1}^\infty \frac{4n^2}{4n^2-1}\). This follows from the ratio of the reduction formulas: \(I_{2n}/I_{2n+1} \to 1\) and the explicit formulas \(I_{2n} = \frac{(2n-1)!!}{(2n)!!}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2}\), \(I_{2n+1} = \frac{(2n)!!}{(2n+1)!!}\). Setting \(I_{2n}\cdot I_{2n+1} = \frac{\pi}{2(2n+1)}\) and taking limits yields Wallis's formula.

questiontext.text: If \(G\) is an antiderivative of a continuous function \(f\) on \([a,b]\) (i.e., \(G'=f\)), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = G(b) - G(a)\)

FTC Part 2: if \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\) and \(G\) is any antiderivative (\(G'=f\)), then \(\int_a^b f = G(b)-G(a)\). This follows because by FTC Part 1, \(F(x)=\int_a^x f\) is also an antiderivative, and any two antiderivatives differ by a constant: \(G=F+C\). So \(G(b)-G(a) = F(b)+C-(F(a)+C) = F(b)-F(a) = \int_a^b f\).
